Provider Prior Authorization · Guided Walkthrough

From intake to report

What actually happens between assembling a prior-auth packet and getting a decision-ready report — explained without a line of code, using one real orthopedic case.

Needs ReviewGate 1 · Provider

Prior Auth Packet · Outpatient Lumbar Fusion

Patient
Thomas Reed · DOB 1966-08-27
Ordering Provider
Meghan Osei, MD · NPI 1669542008
Payer
UnitedHealthcare Commercial HMO
Place of Service
Ambulatory Surgery Center
Diagnosis (ICD-10)
M43.16 · M54.16 · M48.062
Procedure (CPT)
22612 · 22840

In one minute

One button. Four reviewers. A decision-ready report.

You assemble a packet and press one button. About 90 seconds later you get a Submission Readiness Assessment — a clear verdict, the evidence behind it, a checklist of anything still missing, and a polished PDF report for the chart, the surgeon, or your audit trail. Behind the button, a team of four reviewers checks the packet the way an experienced human team would. The tool never submits to a payer and always needs a human sign-off. In this case the verdict was Needs Review: the clinical story was strong and the codes matched policy, but the provider's NPI couldn't be verified — exactly the kind of avoidable problem this is built to catch before a denial.

The reviewers

A four-specialist review

Each reviewer has one job and produces its own findings. The first two work at the same time; the third uses the clinical findings; the last weighs everything and makes the call.

01

Documentation Completeness

"Is the packet complete?"

Runs a checklist over every required field and the clinical narrative, and flags anything missing or malformed.

02

Clinical Evidence

"Is the story strong, the codes valid?"

Reads the notes, confirms every diagnosis code is real and billable, and extracts the clinical picture.

03

Policy Matching

"Does it meet policy? Is the provider real?"

Verifies the provider, matches the case to coverage policy, and builds a per-code coverage matrix.

04

Submission Readiness

"Ready — yes, no, or needs review?"

Makes the final call through three strict gates, in order, and writes the rationale.

The journey

Step by step

01Build the packet 02Press Assess 03Reviewers at work 04Read the assessment 05Human decision 06The PDF report

Step 01

Build the intake packet

Fill the intake form by hand, or press Load Sample for a realistic case. Plain fields capture the patient, the provider's NPI, the payer and plan, the attached documents, the prior-treatment history, and the diagnosis and procedure codes. An advanced section mirrors the discrete fields an EHR or referral system carries.

Step 02

Press "Assess" and watch it work

Submitting kicks off the review. A quick format check catches obvious code typos first, then the four reviewers run and report in live — about 90 seconds end to end. There's nothing to do but watch.

Step 03

The reviewers go to work

Each reviewer checks live, trustworthy sources rather than guessing: the official 2026 diagnosis-code set, the national provider registry, Medicare coverage policy, and the medical literature. The Policy Matching reviewer is where this case turns — it verifies the provider, matches every code to policy, and assesses each coverage criterion.

Step 04

Read the on-screen assessment

The verdict is decided by three strict gates, in order. If an early gate fails, the later ones don't apply — so a single real blocker can't be buried under otherwise-good findings.

Gate 1
Provider CredentialIs the NPI valid and active in the registry?
Fail
Gate 2
Code & Order ValidationAre all diagnosis and procedure codes valid and billable?
N/A
Gate 3
Payer Policy RequirementsAre all requirements met with sufficient evidence?
N/A

For technical viewers

A Debug Console sits next to the Assessment view — a timeline, an event inspector with the raw (privacy-redacted) request/response data, the reviewer→tool graph, and links to the platform traces. It's optional; the Assessment view has everything a coordinator needs.

Step 05

Human decision: accept or revise

Nothing is final until a person signs off. The reviewer enters their name and chooses Accept or Revise. The tool advises; the staff member decides — and the audit trail records who, when, and on what evidence.

Step 06

The PDF report

Finally, the tool generates a clean, file-ready PDF — the same assessment, packaged for the chart, the surgeon, or compliance. Every page is watermarked "AI-assisted draft — human review required before submission." It has eight sections:

1
Executive Summary
Verdict, patient, provider, codes, confidence, plain-English summary.
2
Clinical Evidence
Provider status, matched policies, per-code matrix, evidence from the notes.
3
Criterion Evaluation
Each policy criterion, its status, confidence, and key evidence.
4
Validation Checks
Provider credential, code validation, documentation checklist.
5
Readiness Rationale
The full reasoning, including which gate failed and why.
6
Documentation Gaps
The prioritized to-do list — Critical vs. Non-critical, each with an action.
7
Audit Trail
Data sources consulted, timestamps, confidence scores.
8
Compliance Notes
The rules the assessment followed (provider check, human review).

The verdict, in plain terms

Why "Needs Review"?

Strong case, one hard blocker.

The clinical story and the coding were strong — codes valid and covered, documentation complete, policy criteria met. But the provider's NPI 1669542008 failed validation and couldn't be confirmed in the national registry. The strict gate sequence treats an unverified provider as a hard stop, so the packet is held — not denied.

What staff do next: confirm the correct 10-digit NPI for the provider, obtain the plan-specific UnitedHealthcare commercial policy (the tool matched Medicare policy as a proxy), then re-run. Fix those and the case clears Gate 1 and proceeds.

Reading the screens

What the badges mean

pass — the check succeeded warning — worth a look, not a blocker fail — blocks readiness met / not met — a policy criterion N/A — gate not reached

Good to know

What it is — and isn't

  • A draft, not a decision. It prepares a submission; it is not a payer coverage determination or medical advice.
  • Human sign-off required before anything reaches a payer. The tool can't and doesn't submit.
  • Medicare policy as a proxy. Commercial and Medicare Advantage plans may differ — hence the "get the plan-specific policy" gap.
  • Patient data is protected in the records kept for traceability.

Plain-English terms

Glossary

NPI / NPPES
A provider's 10-digit ID, and the national registry where it's verified.
ICD-10 · CPT / HCPCS
Standard diagnosis codes and procedure codes.
LCD / NCD
Medicare's local/national policies for what's covered, and when.
Conservative care
Non-surgical treatment (e.g., physical therapy, injections) usually required before surgery is approved.

AI-assisted draft for prior-authorization preparation. Payer policy matching reflects Medicare LCDs/NCDs; commercial and Medicare Advantage plans may differ. Human review is required before submission to a payer.

Screenshots are from a live run of the current application using the Orthopedics sample case. Because the reviewers reason in real time, the exact confidence score and criteria counts vary slightly run to run; the verdict and its cause are consistent.
